text: We used a two-port approach in the mid-axillary line through the fourth intercostal space.
After selective right lung ventilation was initiated, pleural adhesions were cauterized in order to find the pericardium and the phrenic nerve.
When the pericardium and phrenic nerve were clearly identified, the lung was carefully pushed backwards in order to approach the pericardium.
The pericardium was opened parallel and 2 cm posterior to the left phrenic nerve.
Once inside the pericardial cavity, the aneurysm of the giant left atrial appendage was immediately revealed.
The giant dimensions of the aneurysm made necessary a large pericardial incision.
When the aneurysm was fully exposed, we inspected its whole circumference in order to find its base.
Small interpericardial adhesions were dissected as can be seen with these small adhesions between the aneurysm and the pulmonary vein.
After successfully removing all adhesions the articulating endoscopic stapler was carefully positioned at the base of the left atrial appendage.
When we were certain that the stapler was covering all of the appendage base we cut off the appendage.
We found a small portion of the appendage still adhering to its base.
We cauterize this adhesion in order to remove the whole aneurysm.
Here we can see the appendage with its giant aneurysm.
When verifying the suture we noticed that the appendage was not fully resected.
To remove this remaining small portion we used an additional stapler.
After being sure that the appendage was fully resected, we carefully verified there was no bleeding at the suture.
We opted not to close the pericardium and left one thoracic drain.
After closure we can see our minimally invasive two-port approach.
